stem: A materials science dataset contains 150 observations of tensile strength. The data is believed to come from a mixture of two populations: bulk material (mean ≈ 63.3 MPa, SD ≈ 8.7) and surface-treated material (mean ≈ 128.2 MPa, SD ≈ 20.4). The proportion of bulk material in the sample is uncertain, estimated between 42% and 63%. The observed overall mean is 93.8 MPa with SD = 35.8. Estimate the true population mean of tensile strength, accounting for the uncertainty in the mixing proportion.

ground_truth.answer: Mixture mean = w×63.3 + (1-w)×128.2. As w varies from 0.42 to 0.63, the mixture mean ranges from 87.3 to 100.9. With sampling variability, CI = [81.6, 106.6]. Point estimate at w=0.53: 93.8 MPa.

solution_steps: Mixture mean formula: μ_mix = w × μ_A + (1-w) × μ_B.
At w=0.42: μ_mix = 100.9 (more of surface-treated material).
At w=0.63: μ_mix = 87.3 (more of bulk material).
Combined interval [81.6, 106.6] accounts for both mixing uncertainty and sampling variability.
common_failure_modes: Using the simple average of the two component means without weighting
Treating the mixing proportion as known instead of uncertain
Ignoring that the overall SD (35.8) is inflated by the between-component variation
Not recognizing that the mixture variance includes both within-component and between-component variation
Failing to propagate the uncertainty in mixing weights through to the final interval
